old_exact: ."Probable cause to issue a search warrant exists when the facts and circumstances relied upon by the judge who issues the warrant would lead a person of reasonable caution to believe the contraband or evidence sought probably will be found in the place to be searched."

source_quote:
conclusion that probable cause exists if there is a substantial basis for that conclusion.
Id. "Probable cause to issue a search warrant exists when the facts and circumstances
relied upon by the judge who issues the warrant would lead a person of reasonable
caution to believe the contraband or evidence sought probably will be found in the
place to be searched." Biwer, 2018 ND 185, ¶ 5, 915 N.W.2d 837.
note: The DB text has a missing space between period and opening quote: ." instead of . ". The source PDF shows 'Id. "Probable' (with space), and when the citation 'Id.' is rendered in markdown format with line breaks, the space between the period ending the preceding sentence and the opening quote must be preserved. This is a standard English typography rule: a space follows a terminal period before an opening quotation mark.

evidence: DB contains compact four-dot ellipsis (....)  after 'exception' in a quoted passage; PDF source shows spaced form (. . . .) which is ND court's published standard ellipsis format. The quotation is from Riley v. California (Supreme Court citation) in ¶12 of the opinion. Verified byte-exact: old_exact appears exactly once in DB text.

source_quote: be employed—it and they are mutually exclusive. Yet
evidence: The authoritative PDF source shows an em-dash (—) between 'employed' and 'it', but the DB contains a regular hyphen (-). This is a common OCR conversion error where a typographic em-dash is misread as a hyphen. The phrase appears in a block quote from an earlier ND case describing limitations of Rule 60(b)(6). The em-dash connects the complete phrase 'employed—it' meaning the clause logically continues ('employed, and it [they]...'). A single occurrence in the opinion body, byte-exact match confirmed.
note: This is the only text-content difference found between the DB text and the authoritative PDF source. The difference in line/blank-line counts is formatting only (the DB uses markdown italics with asterisks for case citations; the source has plain text formatting with typeface on PDF). All numbers, amounts, dates, and legal citations are identical.

e confidence in the outcome—his decision to admit
to the alleged violations
evidence: PDF source (pdftotext output) shows em-dash (U+2014) between 'outcome' and 'his'; DB contains ASCII hyphen (U+0045). Character-verified using Python ord() checks: PDF char ord=8212, DB char ord=45. This is the only substantive character-level difference in the opinion text when all whitespace and citation formatting artifacts are normalized. All proper names, dates, citations, paragraph structure, and substantive text are identical.
note: This is punctuation (em-dash) connecting two independent clauses, not a quote character. The em-dash is the correct form in professional typesetting; the straight hyphen appears to be an OCR misrecognition during PDF-to-text ingestion.
